skill.name: Building LLM-Powered Applications with Claude
skill.description: Comprehensive guide for building LLM-powered applications with the Claude API and Anthropic SDK across Python, TypeScript, Java, Go, Ruby, C#, PHP, and cURL.
skill.summary: End-to-end documentation for Claude API integration, from single API calls to managed agents.
skill.author: anthropics

security_audit.summary: This is an official Anthropic skill from github.com/anthropics/skills providing documentation and code examples for Claude API integration. The skill contains no executable code, no network calls, no filesystem operations, and no environment variable access within the skill itself. All risk factors flagged by static analysis refer to API usage patterns documented in the code examples, not malicious behavior. The skill is safe for publication.

content.user_title: Build LLM applications with the Claude API
content.value_statement: Developers struggle with complex LLM API integration across multiple languages and frameworks. This skill provides official Anthropic documentation, code examples, and best practices for building production-ready applications with the Claude API.
actual_capabilities: Generate code for Claude API calls in 8 languages and raw HTTP
Configure tool use with SDK-specific helpers for automatic loop management
Set up Managed Agents with server-side execution and file mounts
Implement prompt caching with placement guidance and invalidation auditing
Handle streaming, batching, and compaction for production workloads
Select the right surface: single call, workflow, or managed agent
limitations: C# does not support Managed Agents or class-annotation-based tool runners
Managed Agents is available only on Anthropic first-party infrastructure, not on third-party providers
Prompt caching requires a minimum prefix of approximately 1024 tokens to be effective
Opus 4.6 removes support for assistant message prefills which must be replaced with structured outputs

use_cases.title: Single API call for classification and extraction
use_cases.description: Use the Claude API for straightforward text tasks such as document classification, data extraction, summarization, or question answering with a single request and response cycle.
use_cases.target_user: Backend developers integrating AI features into existing applications

use_cases.title: Workflow with custom tool orchestration
use_cases.description: Build multi-step pipelines where your code controls the execution loop and Claude uses your custom tools for API calls, database queries, or file processing.
use_cases.target_user: Full-stack engineers building automated data processing pipelines

use_cases.title: Server-managed stateful agent with workspace
use_cases.description: Deploy a persistent agent configuration with per-session containers for file operations, bash execution, and code running, all managed by Anthropic infrastructure.
use_cases.target_user: Platform teams building developer productivity tools and coding assistants

prompt_templates.title: Send a basic message to Claude
prompt_templates.prompt: Add a Claude API call that asks Claude to summarize the text in this file and prints the response. Use the Anthropic SDK for the project language with Claude Opus 4.6 and adaptive thinking.
prompt_templates.scenario: Getting started with a single API call

prompt_templates.title: Stream a long response
prompt_templates.prompt: Modify this API call to stream the response so the UI can display tokens as they arrive. Use the SDK streaming helper and set max_tokens to 64000 for long outputs.
prompt_templates.scenario: Building a chat interface with real-time display

prompt_templates.title: Implement tool use with the SDK tool runner
prompt_templates.prompt: Add a tool definition for a web search function and wire it up using the Anthropic SDK tool runner so Claude can call it automatically when needed.
prompt_templates.scenario: Adding custom tools for external data access

prompt_templates.title: Create a Managed Agent with a session
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a Managed Agent configuration with bash and file tools, create a session, and send a message. Store the agent ID for reuse so it is not recreated on every request.
prompt_templates.scenario: Deploying a persistent coding agent with workspace

output_examples.input: Classify this customer support ticket as billing, technical, or account issue: I was charged twice for my subscription this month.
output_examples.output: The Claude API returns a structured classification response. The model identifies the message as a billing issue with high confidence. The response includes the classification label, a brief explanation, and any extracted entities such as subscription identifiers or dollar amounts mentioned in the ticket.

output_examples.input: Write a Python function that calls the Claude API with tool use to look up a user in the database.
output_examples.output: The generated code includes the Anthropic SDK import, a tool definition with JSON schema for the database lookup function, and the tool runner setup that automatically executes the function when Claude requests it. The code uses adaptive thinking and streaming for reliability.

best_practices: Default to Claude Opus 4.6 with adaptive thinking for most tasks. Use streaming for requests that may produce long output to avoid HTTP timeouts.
Keep system prompts and tool definitions stable for prompt caching. Place volatile content such as timestamps after the last cache breakpoint. Verify cache hits with usage.cache_read_input_tokens.
Use the official Anthropic SDK tool runner instead of implementing the tool loop manually. It handles retries, error recovery, and continuation automatically.
anti_patterns: Do not define custom types for SDK data structures. The Anthropic SDK exports typed interfaces for all API objects and redefining them loses type safety.
Do not fall back to OpenAI-compatible shims or raw HTTP in projects that already use an official Anthropic SDK. Mixing approaches introduces inconsistency.
Do not silently truncate long inputs when they exceed the context window. Notify the user and discuss alternatives such as chunking or summarization.

faq.question: Which Claude model should I use by default?
faq.answer: Use Claude Opus 4.6 (model ID: claude-opus-4-6) with adaptive thinking enabled. Only use a different model when the user explicitly requests it.

faq.question: What is the difference between the Claude API and Managed Agents?
faq.answer: The Claude API gives you full control over the request loop and tool execution. Managed Agents lets Anthropic run the agent loop and host a per-session container for tool execution, with persistent agent configurations and streaming event output.

faq.question: Why is my prompt cache not being used?
faq.answer: Common causes include volatile content such as timestamps in the system prompt, unsorted JSON data, or a varying tool set. Place stable content first and verify cache hits with the cache_read_input_tokens usage metric.

faq.question: Does Managed Agents work with third-party providers?
faq.answer: No. Managed Agents is available only on Anthropic first-party infrastructure. For deployments on Amazon Bedrock, Google Vertex AI, or Microsoft Foundry, use the Claude API with tool use instead.

faq.question: How do I handle responses that exceed the context window?
faq.answer: Enable server-side compaction on Opus 4.6 or Sonnet 4.6. Append the full response.content back to your messages on every turn, not just the text, to preserve compaction state.

faq.question: What languages are supported by this skill?
faq.answer: This skill covers Python, TypeScript, Java, Go, Ruby, C#, PHP, and raw cURL HTTP. Each language has dedicated documentation with code examples for the Claude API and most have Managed Agents support.
